name: Gemini 3.1 Pro

price.provider: Google AI Studio / Vertex AI (Gemini API)

note: Coding-arena leader with extended long-context frontier reasoning; ARC-AGI-2 77.1%, 1M-token context.

results.notes: ARC-AGI-2, verified score, more than double Gemini 3 Pro
Humanity's Last Exam, no tools (51.4% with search+code execution). Confirmed on both deepmind.google/models/gemini/pro/ and deepmind.google/models/model-cards/gemini-3-1-pro/.
GDPval-AA, Elo-style rating for expert/professional tasks.
tau2-bench Telecom domain (labeled 'τ2-bench Telecom' on the page). Page also shows a separate 'τ2-bench Retail' score of 90.8%, which has no matching taxonomy slug and was left out.
BrowseComp agentic web-browsing benchmark, Search+Python+Browse tool setting.
Agents' Last Exam (ALE-CLI, Overall leaderboard), Gemini CLI harness, High reasoning effort: Pass rate 15.8% / Score 32.0% (rank 34). Score component used per corpus convention.
MMMLU (Multilingual MMLU), single attempt. Confirmed via direct fetch of the official model page; sits in the same performance table as the 12 benchmarks already tracked but was not yet captured.

τ2-bench Retail domain (labeled 'τ2-bench Retail' on the page, alongside the tracked τ2-bench Telecom score). [taxonomy-gap backfill: this score was already verified in an earlier research round but had no matching benchmark slug at the time; added once the slug was introduced.]
Score extracted from the page's embedded data-target HTML attribute since a plain static fetch only shows client-side-animated placeholder values.

SealedBench sealed capability index — composite of capability, token efficiency, speed and cost; rank 14/28 on the 2026-07-22 run (sealedbench.com). Listed as "Gemini 3.1 Pro Preview" (Google). Evaluated at the highest available thinking setting. Corpus 'Gemini 3.1 Pro' (status: preview) is the model SealedBench lists as 'Gemini 3.1 Pro Preview'.

verificationNotes:
Confirmed real via blog.google (2026-02-19 launch) and deepmind.google/models/gemini/pro/ (fetched directly). All 5 benchmark numbers (GPQA 94.3, ARC-AGI-2 77.1, SWE-bench 80.6, MMMU-Pro 80.5, LiveCodeBench Pro 2887 Elo) literally confirmed on the cited pages. 1M context and $2/$12 pricing confirmed via independent sources. Still in preview as of July 2026 (not yet GA) per multiple July-2026 press coverage — status left as preview, correctly. No changes to record. [2026-07-16] Researched the six missing core-benchmark gaps (mmlu, math, aime, humaneval, livecodebench, mmmu). Checked Google's official Feb-2026 model card (deepmind.google/models/model-cards/gemini-3-1-pro/) and its companion evals-methodology PDF (storage.googleapis.com/deepmind-media/gemini/gemini_3-1_pro_model_evaluation.pdf, downloaded and text-extracted directly), plus Vals AI's independent per-benchmark leaderboard (vals.ai/models/google_gemini-3.1-pro-preview, fetched as raw HTML to read the embedded data-target score attributes since the static page otherwise renders placeholder 0.0% values). Google's official table covers a fixed set of ~16 benchmarks (HLE, ARC-AGI-2, GPQA Diamond, Terminal-Bench 2.0, SWE-Bench Verified/Pro, LiveCodeBench Pro, SciCode, APEX-Agents, GDPval-AA, tau2-bench Retail/Telecom, MCP Atlas, BrowseComp, MMMU-Pro, MMMLU, MRCR v2 128k/1M) and never mentions MMLU, MATH, AIME, or HumanEval by name anywhere in either the model card or the eval methodology doc -- these have been superseded in Google's reporting by MMMLU, LiveCodeBench Pro, and SWE-Bench respectively. Notably even AIME, which sibling model Gemini 3 Pro's model card does report (95.0%), is absent from Gemini 3.1 Pro's table, indicating a deliberate reporting-set change rather than an oversight. Vals AI tracks no plain MMLU, MATH, AIME, or HumanEval benchmark for this model at all -- confirmed via the complete href list of every benchmark tile on the model's Vals AI page (only mmlu_pro, gpqa, lcb, mmmu, swebench, terminal-bench-2-1, and a set of domain-specific finance/legal/medical evals are present; no mmlu, math, aime, or humaneval tile exists). All four (mmlu, math, aime, humaneval) are therefore left omitted_no_evidence. Added livecodebench 88.48% from Vals AI (rank 2/127) since its 'lcb' tile is confirmed via direct page-title fetch to be plain 'LiveCodeBench' (percentage pass rate), not the Pro/Elo variant already tracked. For mmmu: Vals AI's 'MMMU' tile (href /benchmarks/mmmu) was directly fetched and its own page reads 'MMMU Pro' -- i.e. it actually measures the same MMMU-Pro protocol already captured by this model's existing mmmu-pro entry (80.5, self-reported), not base MMMU. No genuine base-MMMU score was found for this model from any source checked, so mmmu is left omitted_no_evidence rather than double-counting a Pro-protocol result under the wrong slug. (This same vals.ai MMMU/MMMU-Pro mislabeling was found to affect several other files elsewhere in the corpus -- flagged separately for a dedicated cleanup pass, not touched here.) [2026-07-17] Second-pass deep research on the four legacy-benchmark gaps (mmlu, math, aime, humaneval), deliberately avoiding the first pass's checklist (official launch page, model card, Epoch AI, Vals AI, LMArena, BenchLM) and instead checking six new source types: 1. Stanford HELM — fetched the actual underlying data files (not just the JS-rendered page) for all four HELM projects: Capabilities (config.js -> release v1.15.0, schema.json model list), Lite (v1.13.0), Classic (v0.4.0), and Instruct (v1.0.0). Gemini 3.1 Pro appears in none of them: Lite's newest Google model is Gemini 2.0 Flash (Experimental, Dec 2024); Classic tops out at ~2023-era models; Instruct has zero Gemini models at all. Capabilities is the only project current enough in principle (last updated 2026) but its own scenario/group list (fetched via GCS: core_scenarios -> gpqa, ifeval, mmlu_pro, omni_math, wildbench) does not include plain MMLU, MATH, AIME, or HumanEval as scenarios for ANY model, so even if Gemini 3.1 Pro were added it wouldn't produce these numbers. HELM is conclusively a dead end for this model. 2. arXiv — queried the arXiv API directly (export.arxiv.org) with phrase+boolean queries pairing "Gemini 3.1 Pro" against MMLU, HumanEval, AIME, and MATH-500. Found and read ~20 genuine hits mentioning the model (e.g. "Perfect score on IPhO 2025 theory by Gemini agent", "Self-Anchoring Calibration Drift..." comparing Claude Sonnet 4.6/Gemini 3.1 Pro/GPT-5.2, "Measuring Black-Box Confidence via Reasoning Trajectories" which evaluates Gemini 3.1 Pro on MedQA-USMLE/GPQA Diamond/MMLU-Pro for a confidence-calibration method rather than reporting a citable leaderboard-style raw accuracy). None of the located papers report a plain MMLU, MATH-500, AIME, or HumanEval accuracy number for this specific model as a baseline. 3. GitHub eval-harness run logs — GitHub code search turned up a genuine independent third-party harness repo, Lore-Hex/trustedrouter-benchmarks (mirrored into Lore-Hex/quill-router's benchmark_scores.json with "source_class": "T" / real methodology notes, e.g. "200-question stride-sampled subset (TIGER-Lab/MMLU-Pro), 10-choice CoT, letter-match; no judge"). Directly listed its results/ directory (230+ files) and confirmed it ran google/gemini-3.1-pro-preview through exactly three panels: ifeval_panel.json, gsm8k_panel.json, and mmlu_pro_panel.json — there is no math500_panel, aime_panel, humaneval_panel, or plain-mmlu_panel file in the repo for any model, so this harness never tested those four benchmarks against this model. Separately found two derivative "aggregator" repos, anadim/llm-benchmark-matrix and gioe/aiq (benchmark_scores.yaml), that DO carry mmlu/math_500/aime_2025/humaneval numbers keyed to gemini-3.1-pro, but every one of those specific rows cites either an already-checked-and-empty page (deepmind.google/models/model-cards/gemini-3-1-pro/, misattributing the MMMLU=92.6 figure to plain MMLU) or SEO content-farm blogs (automatio.ai, digitalapplied.com). Cross-checking the aggregator entries against our own already-confirmed official numbers exposed clear corruption: gioe/aiq's "gemini-3.1-pro-preview" row lists GPQA_Diamond=91.9 and ARC_AGI_2=31.1, both wrong (confirmed official values are 94.3 and 77.1) and matching Gemini 3 Pro's launch-window data instead — i.e. a sibling-model mislabeling, not real 3.1 Pro data. These aggregator numbers were rejected as not credible. 4. Other leaderboards — OpenRouter's /benchmarks and /performance pages render no numeric data on a static fetch (client-side only). LiveBench.ai: pulled the live leaderboard CSV directly from LiveBench's own GitHub repo (LiveBench/livebench.github.io, public/table_2026_06_25.csv) and confirmed "gemini-3.1-pro-preview-high" is a tested row, but LiveBench uses its own non-equivalent task taxonomy (AMPS_Hard, math_comp, olympiad for math; code_completion/code_generation for coding) rather than literal MMLU/MATH-500/AIME/HumanEval, so it cannot be mapped onto these taxonomy slugs. Scale AI SEAL leaderboard: confirmed real SEAL coverage of this model, but only for SWE-bench Pro/Verified, not the four target benchmarks. PapersWithCode: no dedicated leaderboard entries found for this model on the MMLU/MATH/AIME/HumanEval benchmark pages. 5. Non-English sources — ran targeted Chinese, Japanese, and Korean queries. All hits were either localized/mirror SEO sites (gemini-cn.com, gemini-cnblog.com, uravation.com, ai-revolution.co.jp) restating mutually-inconsistent numbers each query (MMLU cited as 90.0%, 91.4%, and 92.6%-conflated-with-MMMLU across three different searches; HumanEval 88.9%; MATH 85.5%) with no stable, checkable citation — the same content-farm pattern seen in English — or one apparently-genuine Chinese Zhihu blog post ("Gemini 3.1 Pro 跑分77，我翻完了 Model Card 觉得没那么简单") that returned HTTP 403 on fetch and whose title indicates it is discussing the already-known ARC-AGI-2 77.1 score, not new MMLU/MATH/AIME/HumanEval data. 6. Sibling models — searched for official comparison tables in rival labs' own launch/system-card materials (OpenAI GPT-5.4, Anthropic Claude Opus 4.6, xAI) that might cite Gemini 3.1 Pro's MMLU/MATH/AIME/HumanEval as a comparison baseline. Found none; all "GPT-5.4 vs Claude Opus 4.6 vs Gemini 3.1 Pro" content was again third-party comparison-blog content (mindstudio.ai, digitalapplied.com, evolink.ai, nxcode.io, apiyi.com, promptquorum.com) with numbers that changed between searches (AIME cited as 100%, 95%, and 91.2% across different queries; MATH cited as 95.1%, 98% GSM8K-conflated) — not a genuine official source. Verdict: checked all 6 new source types (exceeding the required minimum of 3), with primary-source verification wherever the source type had a real underlying dataset (HELM schema.json model/scenario lists, arXiv API results, GitHub repo file trees and actual JSON run-panel contents, LiveBench's own CSV). No credible, independently-citable MMLU, MATH(-500), AIME, or HumanEval score for Gemini 3.1 Pro was found anywhere. All four gaps remain omitted_no_evidence. The one new, confirmed-negative fact worth flagging for a future cleanup pass: at least two public GitHub "benchmark aggregator" repos (anadim/llm-benchmark-matrix, gioe/aiq) contain demonstrably wrong/conflated Gemini-3.1-Pro numbers (GPQA 91.9 vs. true 94.3, ARC-AGI-2 31.1 vs. true 77.1) sourced from SEO blogs — a caution against ever treating GitHub "benchmark matrix" repos as automatically credible without checking their underlying citations. [2026-07-18] Third-pass research on the four persistent legacy-benchmark gaps (mmlu, math, aime, humaneval), using the five NEW source types specified for this round (Wayback Machine, conference talks/keynotes, investor/press materials, GitHub SDK-repo issues/discussions, third-party technical-analysis blogs) rather than repeating either prior round's checklist. 1. WAYBACK MACHINE — genuinely attempted at length but conclusively access-blocked in this environment, not merely "checked and negative." Tried: the archive.org `/wayback/available` API (works, returned a valid closest-snapshot timestamp 20260226070151 for the official blog.google launch post), then repeatedly tried to fetch that actual snapshot plus direct CDX API queries against `web.archive.org` via curl (HTTP and HTTPS, with/without redirects, multiple retries with 20-30s backoff across two separate background-retry batches spanning several minutes) and via the WebFetch tool. Every single attempt returned HTTP 429 "Too Many Requests" from web.archive.org's nginx front end (confirmed via verbose curl showing `x-rl: 0`, i.e. zero rate-limit budget remaining) or an outright refusal from the WebFetch tool itself ("Claude Code is unable to fetch from web.archive.org"). Cross-checked the shared scratchpad directory for evidence from this same sandbox's prior research rounds (other models, other sessions): found ~20 previously-saved `*wayback*.html` / `*archive*.html` files from unrelated past research efforts, and every one of them is exactly 162 bytes containing the identical "429 Too Many Requests" body — i.e. this is a systemic, persistent block on this environment's egress to web.archive.org, not a transient issue specific to this query. Wayback Machine must be recorded as inaccessible/inconclusive for this pass rather than a clean negative. 2. CONFERENCE TALKS / KEYNOTES / PODCASTS — checked the official "AI Release Notes" podcast blog post (blog.google/innovation-and-ai/models-and-research/google-deepmind/ai-release-notes-podcast-demis-hassabis/, Logan Kilpatrick interviewing Demis Hassabis): no benchmark numbers of any kind are cited in the post. Searched for the official "Introducing Gemini 3.1 Pro" and "What's new with Gemini from Google DeepMind" YouTube videos; direct fetch of the YouTube watch pages was blocked by a Google bot-detection interstitial (redirected to google.com/sorry/... CAPTCHA page) which was correctly not bypassed per policy. Search-result snippets for these videos' descriptions/summaries surfaced no MMLU/MATH/AIME/HumanEval figures. No transcript or recap blog post with a benchmark slide showing these four benchmarks was found. 3. INVESTOR / PRESS MATERIALS — Alphabet is the directly relevant publicly-traded parent. Checked (a) the official blog.google Q1 2026 earnings recap post by Sundar Pichai: only qualitative "continues to push the frontier in reasoning" language, no benchmark scores. (b) The full Alphabet Investor Presentation (June 2026) transcript PDF, fetched directly from Alphabet's own IR CDN (s206.q4cdn.com/479360582/files/doc_events/2026/Jun/02/Alphabet-Investor-Presentation-June-2026_-Transcript.pdf) and read in full (10 pages, Sundar Pichai + CFO Anat Ashkenazi remarks) — contains extensive business/financial metrics (CapEx, token-volume, revenue, Cloud backlog) but zero mentions of any AI benchmark score whatsoever (not even the already-confirmed GPQA/ARC-AGI-2 figures), confirming Alphabet's investor communications do not disclose model benchmark data at this level of granularity. No mention of Gemini 3.1 Pro MMLU/MATH/AIME/HumanEval in any investor material found. 4. GITHUB ISSUES / SDK REPO DISCUSSIONS — enumerated google-gemini's official GitHub org repos (gemini-cli, cookbook, gemini-api-quickstart, api-examples, etc.) and searched issues/discussions for MMLU, HumanEval, AIME, and "MATH benchmark" combined with "3.1 Pro" via both `gh search issues` and the GitHub Discussions GraphQL API on google-gemini/gemini-cli (20 most-recent discussions pulled directly) — no maintainer or engineer reply citing any of the four benchmark scores was found; discussion content is entirely about CLI usage, migrations (Antigravity), extensions, and unrelated feature requests. Also inspected the one genuinely on-topic external issue found by search, simonw/llm-gemini#121 ("Gemini 3.1 Pro"), which documents model IDs and a "pelican SVG" qualitative test but contains no benchmark figures. GitHub SDK-repo issues/discussions conclusively yield nothing for this model. 5. THIRD-PARTY TECHNICAL-ANALYSIS BLOGS — checked, by direct fetch, every source type example plus close analogues: Simon Willison's dedicated Feb 19 2026 launch-day post (simonwillison.net/2026/Feb/19/gemini-31-pro/) — pricing/pelican commentary only, no MMLU/MATH/AIME/HumanEval. SemiAnalysis newsletter ("The Coding Assistant Breakdown: More Tokens Please") — discusses MMLU saturation historically but cites no Gemini-3.1-Pro-specific number. Zvi Mowshowitz's Substack ("Gemini 3.1 Pro Aces Benchmarks, I Suppose") — thorough benchmark rundown (ARC-AGI-1/2, LiveBench, Voxelbench, Codeforces Elo, HLE) but explicitly does not include MMLU, MATH, AIME, or HumanEval. SmartScope's dedicated gap-analysis piece ("Behind Gemini 3.1 Pro's '13 out of 16 Wins' — The Benchmarks Published and the Benchmarks Left Out") — directly examines which benchmarks Google chose to publish/omit and still never surfaces MMLU/MATH/AIME/HumanEval values; states "whether Google lacks custom harness results or chose not to publish them remains unclear." Towards AI newsletter (TAI #193) — independent benchmark rundown citing ARC-AGI-2, GPQA, Terminal-Bench, LMSYS Elo, AA-Omniscience, MRCR v2, again with no MMLU/MATH/AIME/HumanEval figure. Artificial Analysis's own AIME-2025 leaderboard page and the Gemini-3.1-Pro-Preview model page were fetched directly: AA has fully migrated away from plain MMLU/HumanEval (offers only MMLU-Pro and Global-MMLU-Lite) and its AIME-2025 leaderboard does not list a Gemini 3.1 Pro row at all. No credible technical-analysis blog reports any of the four target numbers for this model. Verdict: genuinely attempted all 5 of the newly-specified source types (exceeding the required minimum of 3), with 4 of 5 (conference talks, investor materials, GitHub SDK repos, third-party analysis blogs) yielding conclusive negative evidence via direct primary-source fetches, and the 5th (Wayback Machine) yielding a confirmed, systemic environmental access block rather than a clean negative. Combined with the two prior independent research rounds (round 1: official launch materials/Epoch AI/Vals.ai/LMArena/BenchLM/llm-stats.com; round 2: Stanford HELM/arXiv/GitHub eval-harness logs/MathArena/LiveBench/OpenRouter/Scale SEAL/regional-language sources/sibling-model tables), this is now a third independently-designed search strategy finding zero credible mmlu/math/aime/humaneval scores for Gemini 3.1 Pro. Per the task's own criterion, this constitutes very strong evidence of a genuine, durable absence rather than a search-methodology gap. All four gaps (mmlu, math, aime, humaneval) remain omitted_no_evidence; no new results were added this round. One incidental data point worth flagging for anyone doing a future pass: the search-engine AI-summary layer twice fabricated/misattributed benchmark numbers when asked directly about these four benchmarks (once claiming "94.3% MMLU" and "80.6% HumanEval" — actually this model's real GPQA and SWE-bench scores respectively — and separately claiming AIME/MATH/HumanEval figures of 95%/95%/94% sourced only to known SEO content-farm domains already flagged as unreliable in round 2). Neither was corroborated by any primary source actually fetched and was correctly rejected. [2026-07-18] Fourth-pass research targeting specifically the 'mmmu' (base MMMU, vision-reasoning) gap, per explicit instruction to search harder since this is a current-generation benchmark most active vendors still report (unlike the already-exhausted legacy mmlu/math/aime/humaneval gaps). Modality check first: this model's own corpus fields show modalities=[text,vision,audio,video] and capabilities.vision=true, so MMMU is architecturally APPLICABLE, not inapplicable — absence would have to be a genuine reporting gap, not a modality mismatch. Checked all 6 recommended source types: 1. Official launch materials — re-fetched deepmind.google/models/gemini/pro/ and the model card; both list only MMMU-Pro (80.5%, already tracked), never plain MMMU. Downloaded and text-extracted (pdftotext) the official eval-methodology PDF (storage.googleapis.com/deepmind-media/gemini/gemini_3-1_pro_model_evaluation.pdf) directly: its "Image" methodology bullet reads verbatim "MMMU-Pro scores are averaged across the Standard (10 options) and Vision settings" — no mention of base MMMU anywhere in the 3-page document. Google's official reporting for this model literally never computed a base-MMMU number. 2. Independent trackers — (a) Epoch AI (epoch.ai/models/gemini-3-1-pro): public page shows only the composite Epoch Capabilities Index (155, rank 13/211), no per-benchmark MMMU breakdown. (b) Vals AI: re-verified the prior round's finding independently by curling the raw HTML of both the model page and the benchmark page myself. The model page's "MMMU" tile (href /benchmarks/mmmu) renders a bar with `--target-width:88.208%` in its embedded CSS (i.e. the real underlying score, matching what search-engine AI summaries surface as "88.21% MMMU"), but fetching /benchmarks/mmmu directly shows `<title>MMMU Pro` and `

MMMU Pro

` — Vals AI's "MMMU" tile is a mislabeled alias for their MMMU-Pro protocol, not a genuine base-MMMU score. Using it would double-count/mislabel the already-tracked mmmu-pro result. (c) llm-stats.com: fetched its dedicated MMMU leaderboard (llm-stats.com/benchmarks/mmmu) directly — Gemini 3.1 Pro is NOT listed at all; the newest Gemini row on that leaderboard is Gemini 2.5 Pro Preview 06-05 (0.820), i.e. llm-stats.com never obtained/ran a base-MMMU number for the 3.x generation. Its separate MMMU-Pro leaderboard is where Gemini 3.1 Pro actually appears. (d) Artificial Analysis (artificialanalysis.ai/models/gemini-3-1-pro-preview): evaluation categories list only "MMMU-Pro (Visual reasoning)", no plain MMMU. (e) BenchLM.ai: full 47-benchmark listing for this model contains "MMMU-Pro: 83.9%" and "AA-MMMU-Pro: 82.4%" but explicitly no plain-MMMU entry. (f) Scale SEAL: no MMMU-specific leaderboard coverage for this model found. 3. Academic (arXiv) — search-engine AI-summaries repeatedly asserted numbers like "Gemini 3 Pro attains 92.8% on MMMU" (citing arXiv 2601.06521, "BabyVision") and "81% on MMMU" (various content-farm domains), but these did not survive primary-source verification: direct WebFetch of the BabyVision paper (both PDF and abs page) confirms it evaluates "Gemini3-Pro-Preview" (i.e. Gemini 3 Pro, not 3.1 Pro) and reports only its own BabyVision-specific score (49.7), not MMMU at all — the AI-summary layer fabricated/misattributed both the model identity and the benchmark. A second candidate paper (arXiv 2605.09614, visual-retention reasoning) was also fetched directly and contains no Gemini-3.1-Pro/MMMU pairing. No genuine arXiv baseline table with a base-MMMU score for this exact model was found. 4. GitHub eval-harness logs — re-examined the previously-identified third-party harness repo (Lore-Hex/quill-router) by pulling its full git tree via the GitHub API (838 files): zero files anywhere in the repo contain "mmmu" in the path, confirming this harness never ran an MMMU panel against gemini-3.1-pro-preview (consistent with the prior round's finding that it only ran ifeval/gsm8k/mmlu_pro panels). 5. Sibling-model comparison tables — fetched Google's own Gemini 3.5 Flash model card (deepmind.google/models/model-cards/gemini-3-5-flash/, released after Gemini 3.1 Pro in the same family) and read its full multimodal comparison table: it cites Gemini 3.1 Pro's MMMU-Pro score (80.5%, matching the already-tracked value) alongside Gemini 3.5 Flash/3 Flash/Claude Opus 4.7/Sonnet 4.6/GPT-5.5, but the table has no plain-MMMU row for ANY model — confirming the whole Gemini 3.x/3.5 generation's official reporting uniformly substitutes MMMU-Pro for base MMMU, this is not specific to the 3.1 Pro checkpoint. 6. Modality confirmation — vision is a first-class capability (not inapplicable), so the search was warranted and exhaustive per the task's own instructions. Verdict: genuinely checked all 6 recommended methods (exceeding the 4-method minimum), with primary-source verification at each step (direct PDF text extraction, direct HTML/title fetch of the mislabeled Vals AI tile, direct GitHub tree listing, direct sibling-model-card fetch) rather than relying on search-engine AI summaries, which were repeatedly shown to fabricate or conflate MMMU/MMMU-Pro/wrong-model-version numbers when queried directly (a recurring pattern already flagged in the 2026-07-16 note). Base MMMU is not merely "not yet found" but appears to be a deliberate reporting substitution by Google (MMMU-Pro replacing MMMU) that has propagated through every independent tracker checked — none has computed or sourced an independent base-MMMU number for this model. 'mmmu' remains omitted_no_evidence; no new result added. The existing mmmu-pro entry (80.5, self-reported) is correctly left as the only MMMU-family score for this model.
